title: The Japanese Robe
creator: Stevens, Alfred, 1823-1906
period_styles: Japonisme; realism (artistic form of expression); Late Victorian
description: A woman in a blue kimono stands before a mirror with her back to the viewer and only partially revealing her face as she examines herself.
significance: This work reflects Western fascination with Japanese objects and fashion, especially the kimono, which became symbols of exoticism in European art.

title: Lily
creator: Dow, Arthur W. (Arthur Wesley), 1857-1922
description: A vertically oriented composition of a stylized lily with bold outlines and flattened color fields.
significance: This work reflects the influence of Japanese woodblock prints through its flat color areas, simplified forms, and strong vertical composition.

title: Japanese Man
creator: Shindler, A. Zeno (Antonio Zeno), 1823-1899
description: A traditionally dressed Japanese man, samurai, stands in a landscape holding a sword.
significance: This work reflects cultural exchange by depicting a Japanese figure through a Western oil-painting tradition, combining European realism with distinctly Japanese subject matter like the kimono and sword. Such portraits emerged after Japan opened to global trade in the mid-19th century, when Western artists became fascinated with Japanese people, dress, and identity.

title: Japonisme
creator: Somm, Henry, 1844-1907
cultural_elements: folding fan; paper lantern; bonnet; western dresses
description: A Western woman is juxtaposed with Japanese motifs, people, and architecture.
significance: This work directly represents the cultural exchange between Japan and the West by combining Western figures with Japanese objects like fans and costumes. It illustrates the widespread fascination with Japanese art and design that reshaped Western artistic practices during the Japonisme movement.

title: Water Lily Pond
creator: Monet, Claude, 1840-1926
description: A pond with water lilies and a curved bridge fills the composition.
significance: This work demonstrates how Japanese landscape design influenced Western art, particularly Monet’s creation of a Japanese-style garden with a bridge. His compositions reflect Japanese aesthetics in their emphasis on nature, asymmetry, and atmospheric effects.

title: The Child’s Bath
creator: Cassatt, Mary, 1844-1926
description: A mother washes a child in an intimate domestic scene.
significance: This work shows how Japanese prints influenced Western depictions of everyday life, particularly intimate domestic scenes. Cassatt adopted Japanese techniques like flattened color, strong outlines, and unusual cropping, reshaping her portrayal of women and children.

title: Noh Dancer
creator: La Farge, John, 1880-1963
medium_technique: drawing; wood engraving on Japan tissue
description: A Noh dancer in elaborate costume poses with a folding fan in a theatrical setting.
significance: This work reflects Western fascination with Japanese theatrical traditions, particularly the stylized gestures and costumes of Noh performance, which became known in Europe after Japan opened to trade in the mid-19th century. The emphasis on pose, costume, and symbolic movement mirrors how Western artists engaged with Japanese cultural practices as exotic and visually compelling subjects.

title: The Flowering Orchard
creator: Gogh, Vincent van, 1853-1890
description: A orchard scene rendered with expressive brushwork and simplified forms.
significance: This work demonstrates how Western artists adopted Japanese compositional techniques such as flattened space, bold outlines, and decorative patterning derived from ukiyo-e prints. Van Gogh was deeply influenced by Japanese prints, which introduced new ways of depicting nature with bright color and simplified forms.

title: Flowering Plum Orchard
creator: Gogh, Vincent van, 1853-1890
description: A direct reinterpretation of Hiroshige’s composition with intensified color and added decorative borders.
significance: This work is a direct reinterpretation of a Japanese woodblock print, showing how Western artists actively copied and studied Japanese art to learn its techniques. Van Gogh’s inclusion of Japanese-style borders and calligraphy reflects a deliberate attempt to immerse himself in Japanese visual language.
related_works: The Flowering Orchard; Plum Garden at Kameido

title: Plum Garden at Kameido
creator: Andō, Hiroshige, 1797-1858
description: A close-up composition of a flowering plum tree dominates the foreground, with figures and a garden beyond.
significance: This work represents a traditional Japanese landscape that later became widely circulated in the West, shaping Western perceptions of Japan as poetic and picturesque.

title: Peaceful Pleasures of the Highest Nobility
creator: Inoue, Yasuji, 1864-1889
cultural_elements: western dresses; military uniform; straw hats (sun hats)
depicted_objects: women; children; men; toys; books; furniture; garden
description: A multi-panel composition showing women, men, and children in a transition between exterior to interior  environment.
significance: This work shows Japanese families incorporating Western-style education, fashion, and social practices into everyday life.

title: A Picture of Prosperity: America
creator: Utagawa, Hiroshige, 1826-1869
cultural_elements: western dresses; parasols; walking canes; bonnets; Romanesque Revival
description: A lively urban scene depicts Westernized architecture and fashionable citizens in an American city.
significance: This work reflects Japan’s fascination with Western modernity by depicting an imagined American city filled with large buildings, ships, and active urban life, emphasizing prosperity and technological advancement. Created as part of early encounters with the West, it presents an idealized and somewhat inaccurate vision of America, revealing how Japanese artists constructed their understanding of foreign societies through limited information.

title: America
creator: Hiroshige, III, 1842?-1894
description: Woman in western-style dress and feathered headdress rides a hourse in a snowy landscape beneath pine branches.
significance: This work presents an American figure in a stylized, almost theatrical way, emphasizing exotic clothing and dramatic movement. It reflects both admiration for Western power and a tendency to stereotype foreigners as visually striking and unfamiliar
related_works: An American Drawn from Life; View of the Seafront in Yokohama

title: View of the Seafront in Yokohama
creator: Hiroshige, III, 1842?-1894
cultural_elements: fishing vessels; naval ships; carrioles; western dresses; western flags; English language
depicted_objects: harbor scene; ports; watercrafts; flags; horses
description: A bustling harbor scene shows foreign ships, Western-style buildings, and mixed populations in the treaty port of Yokohama.
significance: This work shows Western ships, architecture, and flags in a Japanese port, emphasizing curiosity and excitement about foreign technology and trade. Such images document how the arrival of Western powers transformed Japanese cities and signaled a shift toward modernization and global exchange.

title: French
creator: Utagawa, Yoshikazu, active 1848-1863
cultural_elements: bonnets; western dresses; cranes; tea services
description: A Western couple sits indoors with tea service and furnishings.
significance: This work depicts Europeans in a stylized and somewhat exaggerated manner, revealing how Japanese artists formed early stereotypes of Western appearance and behavior. The unfamiliar clothing, gestures, and setting highlight both curiosity and a sense of cultural distance.

title: In The Mood for a Walk
creator: Taiso, Yoshitoshi, 1839-1892
cultural_elements: straw hats (sun hats); western dresses; parasols; tassel; buttons; irises
description: Japanese woman in hybrid Western-influenced dress strolls among irises.
significance: This work reflects Japan’s fascination with Western fashion during the Meiji period, as women are shown wearing European-style clothing alongside traditional hairstyles and poses. It demonstrates how Western dress became a symbol of modernity and social change as Japan rapidly opened to foreign influence after 1868.

title: An American Drawn from Life
creator: Utagawa, Yoshitora, active 1850-1870
description: A bearded Western man rides a horse in military-style attire.
significance: This work portrays a Western figure with exaggerated features and attire, reflecting how Japanese artists interpreted foreigners through limited exposure and observation. It suggests both fascination and misunderstanding, as Western identity becomes a subject of visual study in Japan.

title: Dutchmen
creator: Utagawa, Yoshitora, active 1850-1870
description: Two European figures read and converse in an interior setting, rendered with attention to books, furniture, and dress.
significance: This work highlights Japan’s interest in Western individuals by depicting a domestic scene of reading. The detailed rendering of clothing and posture suggests admiration, while slight exaggerations, such as the blue eyes, reveal fascination with different features.
related_works: French; An American Drawn from Life
